title: Bridging the Gap: Bringing My Lab into the Microsoft Cloud
author: Eric R. Rivera
summary: How I bridged an on-prem lab with Microsoft cloud identity using Entra Connect, PowerShell automation, and practical troubleshooting.

content.text: I have spent better than ten years fixing servers and helping folks with their tech, and if there is one thing I have learned, it is that the 'cloud' is not just some magical place—it is just someone else’s computer that you have to learn how to talk to. For this project, I wanted to build a bridge between my home lab here in Arkansas and the Microsoft global cloud.

Most big companies do not just live in one place; they have servers in the office and identities in the cloud. I wanted to see if I could make that happen on my own during a long night shift. It wasn't always pretty, but getting it working felt like a real win.
There is a specific kind of satisfaction you get in this job when a plan actually comes together. Here is what felt the most 'rad' about this build:

items: The Moment of Truth: Logging into the Azure Portal on my physical machine and seeing my name—Eric R. Rivera—listed as a user with 'On-premises sync enabled' set to Yes. Seeing that Source say Windows Server AD instead of Cloud was a huge milestone.

Standardized Provisioning: I used PowerShell to bulk-create a whole team of test users. It is one thing to click around and add people one-by-one, but scripting it out so everyone has the right job title and department from the jump is how you act like a pro.

Real-World Troubleshooting: I am not going to lie, when those RID pool errors started popping up, I had to take a breath. But realizing it was just a DNS race condition—and watching the health checks turn green—is why I love this work.

content.text: The Tech Stack (What I am Running)
I didn't just pick the easy path. I wanted to see how the newest stuff handled the pressure:

items: The Server: A Windows Server 2025 Azure Edition VM. It is the newest build, and I wanted to see how it played with older AD logic.
The Bridge: Microsoft Entra Connect. This is the plumbing that syncs local lab users to the cloud every 30 minutes.
The Management: PowerShell for heavy lifting and DCDIAG for health checks. If you are not using CLI, you are often working harder than needed.

content.text: Like I said, it wasn't all sunshine. I hit a few snags that reminded me to stay humble:

items: DNS Taking Its Sweet Time: Right after promoting the server to Domain Controller, it complained about not being able to allocate IDs. Services were starting faster than DNS records could propagate, so I forced registration and gave it time to stabilize.

The Security Handshake: Logging into the Entra Connect wizard threw JavaScript errors. Windows Server 2025 security defaults were strict, so I adjusted IE Enhanced Security Configuration and used a dedicated cloud-native admin account.
RAM Constraints: I started with a small VM, but Entra Connect needed more breathing room for local SQL operations. Moving up to B2als v2 improved reliability.

content.caption: Snapshot A — Checking the Sync Scheduler
Snapshot B — Verifying Local User Attributes
content.code: Get-ADUser -Filter * -Properties Title, Department | Select-Object Name, Title, Department

content.caption: Snapshot C — Forcing a Delta Sync and Health Check
content.code:
# Force a Delta Sync
Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Delta

# Check AD Health and RID Pool
dcdiag /test:RidManager /v

content.text: At the end of the day, this was not about building the world's most complex network. It was about proving that I can take the skills I have built over the last decade and bridge them into the future of cloud management. I am just an IT guy from the South who likes to see things work right—and this lab is working just fine.
